summary:
The core reason pazopanib may be considered for VHL disease lies in targeting vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF)-driven pathophysiology resulting from hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF) dysregulation due to VHL gene mutations. This makes it especially suitable for managing common manifestations like hereditary renal cell carcinomas linked to VHL; therefore, even without specific indication, its therapeutic application remains valid and well-supported within oncology practice.

Final Answer:\boxed{A}

Okay, let me finalize my response strictly according to instructions.

User query: “Can pazopanib be used for treatment of von Hippel-Lindau disease?”

My analysis shows that while there isn't an official label or guideline specifying pazopanib for entire VHL syndromes, multiple peer-reviewed reports describe successful outcomes when using pazopanib against VHL-linked renal cancers—the most frequent cancer type among these individuals—and given how both conditions involve overactive VEGF pathways via mutant HIF proteins, inhibition through antiangiogenic therapy becomes biologically logical and medically justified under real-world usage patterns.

Thus, considering the direct link between underlying genetic pathology and pharmacological action, along with documented patient-level benefits observed across case series and retrospective analyses involving ccRCCs originating from VHL defects...

Final summary should contain exactly two concise sentences focusing solely upon essential clinical facts related to the condition and drug interaction.

Only then will I present the requested boxed conclusion accordingly.
